text: This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: 这里是BBC英语教学的《六分钟英语》节目。

text: I'm Neil.
zh: 我是尼尔。

text: And I'm Beth.
zh: 我是贝丝。

text: In Britain we call them crisps and you might know them as potato chips.
zh: 在英国我们叫它crisps,你可能知道它叫potato chips(薯片)。

text: But whatever you call them, these fried potato slices are probably the world's favourite snack.
zh: 但不管你怎么称呼它,这种油炸薯片大概是世界上最受欢迎的零食了。

text: Neil, are you a big crisp eater and what's your favourite flavour?
zh: 尼尔,你很爱吃薯片吗?你最喜欢什么口味?

text: I love crisps, Beth.
zh: 我很爱薯片,贝丝。

text: They are my favourite snack.
zh: 它们是我最喜欢的零食。

text: My favourite flavour is prawn cocktail, which is controversial because a lot of people don't like that flavour.
zh: 我最喜欢的口味是鸡尾酒虾味,这个说法有点有争议,因为很多人并不喜欢这个口味。

text: I think my favourite is salt and vinegar.
zh: 我觉得我最喜欢的是盐醋味。

text: Yummy.
zh: 好好吃啊。

text: You're making me hungry.
zh: 你说得我都饿了。

text: Well, crisps might be popular, but with lots of oil and salt, they're not the healthiest food.
zh: 不过薯片虽然很受欢迎,但因为含大量油和盐,并不是最健康的食物。

text: So why do we love them so much?
zh: 那我们为什么这么爱吃薯片呢?

text: That's what BBC World Service programme The Food Chain wanted to find out.
zh: 这正是BBC世界服务节目《The Food Chain》想要探究的问题。

text: Here, presenter Ruth Alexander talks to Mitchell Dingwall, a food executive responsible for developing new crisp flavours.
zh: 主持人露丝·亚历山大在这里采访了负责开发新薯片口味的食品高管米切尔·丁沃尔。

text: Why do we love crisps so much?
zh: 我们为什么这么爱吃薯片?

text: I think it's just that crunch, right?
zh: 我觉得就是那种脆感,对吧?

text: You can't beat the crunch and then that delicious salty feeling in your mouth and it just becomes a snowball effect.
zh: 那种酥脆无与伦比,再加上嘴里那种美味的咸香感,就形成了一种滚雪球效应。

text: You just want more of it.
zh: 你就会越吃越想吃。

text: For Mitchell, the best thing about crisps is the loud crunch.
zh: 对米切尔来说,薯片最棒的地方就是那响亮的咔嚓声。

text: He says you can't beat it, An idiom meaning something like that's the best thing and you won't find anything better.
zh: 他说“你无法打败它”,这是一个习语,意思是这就是最好的,你找不到比这更好的了。

text: Mitchell says once you've started eating crisps, it's hard to stop and you just want to eat more and more.
zh: 米切尔说,一旦你开始吃薯片,就很难停下来,只会越吃越多。

text: He calls this a snowball effect, a situation where doing something causes other similar things to happen.
zh: 他把这称为“滚雪球效应”,指做一件事引发了一连串类似的事情发生。

text: You eat one crisp and before you know it, the whole packet's gone.
zh: 你吃一片薯片,不知不觉整包就吃光了。

text: In this episode, we'll be visiting two countries in love with crisps.
zh: 在这一集里,我们将访问两个热爱薯片的国家。

text: And as usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases, all of which you can find on our website, bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 像往常一样,我们会学习一些有用的新单词和短语,这些都可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到。

text: But first, I have a question for you, Beth.
zh: 不过首先,我有个问题要问你,贝丝。

text: The record for the world's largest packet of crisps was recently broken in Saudi Arabia, but how much did the packet weigh?
zh: 世界最大薯片包装的纪录最近在沙特阿拉伯被打破,那么这包薯片有多重呢?

text: Was it a.
zh: 是a.

text: about 1 .5 tonnes, b.
zh: 大约1.5吨,

text: about 2 .5 tonnes, or c.
zh: b. 大约2.5吨,

text: about 3 .5 tonnes?
zh: 还是c. 大约3.5吨?

text: OK, wow, they are all huge.
zh: 好吧,哇,这些数字都很惊人。

text: I'm going to guess about 3 .5 tonnes.
zh: 我猜大概是3.5吨。

text: OK, we'll find out the answer later.
zh: 好的,我们稍后揭晓答案。

text: Let's travel first to Singapore, home of Jolene Ng, a market research analyst who specialises in salty snacks.
zh: 我们先去新加坡看看,那里住着乔琳·吴,一位专门研究咸味零食的市场调研分析师。

text: Here's Jolene talking with Ruth Alexander for BBC programme The Food Chain.
zh: 下面是乔琳与露丝·亚历山大在BBC节目《The Food Chain》中的对话。

text: Is the potato chip the most popular of all salty snacks?
zh: 薯片是所有咸味零食中最受欢迎的吗?

text: It's very, very much dependent on personal preference.
zh: 这非常取决于个人喜好。

text: But if I were to look at certain countries, you could see like in the UK, it's very much part and parcel of life.
zh: 但如果看某些国家的话,比如在英国,薯片几乎是生活中不可或缺的一部分。

text: You don't have it only during snack times.
zh: 不只是在零食时间才吃。

text: You have it with lunch as well.
zh: 午餐时也会吃。

text: You put it, my husband's from UK and he kind of put it in his sandwich as well.
zh: 我丈夫是英国人,他甚至会把薯片放进三明治里。

text: Oh yeah, a cheese sandwich with a crisp inside.
zh: 哦对,一个夹着薯片的奶酪三明治。

text: Oh yeah, that's great.
zh: 哦是啊,太棒了。

text: Exactly.
zh: 没错。

text: Jolene says the popularity of different snacks depends on personal preference.
zh: 乔琳说,不同零食的受欢迎程度取决于个人喜好。

text: Someone's subjective liking or disliking for something based on their personal opinion.
zh: 这是指一个人基于自身看法而产生的主观喜恶。

text: In some places, crisps are part and parcel of everyday life.
zh: 在一些地方,薯片是日常生活中不可或缺的一部分。

text: Part and parcel means to be an essential element or feature of something.
zh: “part and parcel”意思是某事物必不可少的组成部分。

text: In Britain, crisps are so essential that people even eat crisp sandwiches.
zh: 在英国,薯片如此重要,以至于人们甚至会吃薯片三明治。

text: But not everywhere is so in love with potato chips.
zh: 但并不是每个地方都这么钟爱薯片。

text: Let's meet Jenny Martinez, a food blogger from Mexico.
zh: 我们来认识一下珍妮·马丁内斯,一位来自墨西哥的美食博主。

text: In Mexico, tortilla corn chips are eaten instead of potato chips, and it's common to see street vendors selling bags of corn tortillas filled with different sauces.
zh: 在墨西哥,人们吃的是玉米片而不是薯片,街头小贩常常售卖装满各种酱料的玉米片袋。

text: Here, Jenny tells Ruth Alexander more for BBC World Service programme, The Food Chain.
zh: 下面,珍妮在BBC世界服务节目《The Food Chain》中向露丝·亚历山大做了更多介绍。

text: For you, which is your go -to snack, the tortilla chip or the potato chip?
zh: 对你来说,你首选的零食是玉米片还是薯片?

text: Definitely the tortilla chip.
zh: 绝对是玉米片。

text: Why?
zh: 为什么?

text: Because you could do so many things, dip it in almost everything,
zh: 因为你可以拿它蘸几乎任何东西,

text: adding some guacamole, spicy salsa or just sour cream with a mixture of chipotle sauce.
zh: 加点鳄梨酱、辣莎莎酱,或者加了奇波雷辣酱的酸奶油。

text: Ruth asks if tortillas are Jenny's go -to snack.
zh: 露丝问珍妮,玉米片是不是她的首选零食。

text: You can use the phrase go -to to describe something which is relied upon to be the best thing for a particular purpose.
zh: “go-to”这个短语可以用来形容某样东西被公认为是完成某事的最佳选择。

text: For example, for a lot of people, Wikipedia is the go -to website for online information.
zh: 比如,对很多人来说,维基百科是获取网络信息的首选网站。

text: And Jenny thinks corn tortillas are especially good with guacamole,
zh: 珍妮认为玉米片配上鳄梨酱特别好吃,

text: a paste made of mashed avocado, which sounds delicious and may be a little healthier than a British crisp sandwich.
zh: 鳄梨酱是用捣碎的牛油果做成的酱料,听起来很美味,或许比英式薯片三明治还要健康一点。

text: Speaking of which, what was the answer to your question, Neil?
zh: 说到这个,尼尔,你那个问题的答案是什么?

text: I asked you how heavy the world's largest bag of crisps was.
zh: 我之前问你世界最大的薯片包有多重。

text: And I said very heavy, over three and a half tonnes.
zh: 我猜的是很重,超过三点五吨。

text: Well, it's not that big, but the real answer is still enormous, about 1 .5 tonnes.
zh: 其实没有那么大,不过真正的答案依然十分惊人,大约是1.5吨。

text: The bag, made in Riyadh, was nine metres tall.
zh: 这个包装在利雅得制造,高达九米。

text: Yeah.
zh: 是啊。

text: OK, let's recap the vocabulary we've learned, starting with the phrase, you can't beat that, meaning that something is the best and you won't find anything better.
zh: 好了,我们来复习一下今天学到的词汇,首先是“you can't beat that”,意思是某样东西是最好的,找不到更好的了。

text: A snowball effect is a situation where one action causes other similar actions to happen.
zh: “滚雪球效应”指的是一个行为引发一连串类似行为发生的情况。

text: Someone's personal preference is their subjective liking or disliking for something based on their own opinion.
zh: 一个人的“personal preference”指的是基于自身看法而产生的主观喜恶。

text: To be part and parcel of something means to be an essential or integral feature of it.
zh: “part and parcel”表示是某事物不可或缺、必不可少的一部分。

text: And something which is described as the go -to can be relied upon as the best thing for a particular situation.
zh: 被称为“go-to”的东西,是指在特定情况下被公认为最佳选择、值得信赖的东西。

text: And finally, guacamole is a paste made of mashed avocado, lime juice and salt.
zh: 最后,“guacamole”(鳄梨酱)是用捣碎的牛油果、青柠汁和盐做成的一种酱料。

text: Delicious.
zh: 太好吃了。

text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but if you're hungry for more, then head over to our website bbclearningenglish .com where you'll find a worksheet and quiz for this episode.
zh: 我们的六分钟又到时间了,如果你还意犹未尽,可以访问我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com,那里有本期的练习题和小测验。

text: See you again soon, but for now, goodbye.
zh: 我们下次再见,现在先说再见。

text: Six Minute English, from bbclearningenglish .com
zh: 《六分钟英语》,来自bbclearningenglish.com。
